result.meeting_context: The Education Enhancement Reserve Joint Powers Board met to review district uses of Shoreline funds, confirm FY22/23 payment amounts, and discuss negotiating a successor agreement before the current JPA expires June 30, 2023. The Board aligned on a short‑term payment guarantee and a Q1 2023 meeting while outlining a path toward a longer-term agreement.

points.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
points.text: The Board approved the December 16, 2021 meeting minutes following a motion by Ayinde Rudolph and a second by Dr. Meyer; no corrections or additions were raised before the vote, and the chair declared the minutes adopted.

points.speaker: Nellie Meyer
points.text: MVLA High School District receives approximately $3.2 million from the reserve. Allocations include $1.124 million for the Freestyle Academy (teacher salaries/benefits), $1.241 million for expanded computer science and engineering classes (teacher salaries/benefits), and $838,255 for technology equipment and support services, including technology staff.
points.source: Nellie Meyer : 1.124 million is currently used to support our Freestyle Academy... It is also used to expand computer science and engineering class... for a total of $1.241 million... 838,255 is used to support technology equipment and other support services.

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Mountain View Whisman School District received a little over $5 million in base and bonus funds and spent about $3.6 million. Funds supported Living Classroom science lessons, technology services, Chromebook software licensing, one‑to‑one device replacements, technology staff salaries/benefits, and administration. Unused funds are reserved for future textbook adoptions.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : So Mountain View Whisperman received a total of a little over $5,000,000 in base and bonus funds and spent a total of 3.6.

points.speaker: Laura Blakely
points.text: For unspent funds, the district reserves portions for textbook adoptions, including an upcoming math adoption. Because textbook adoptions cost $2–$3 million each, the district saves across multiple years to cover these expenditures without disrupting operations.
points.source: Laura Blakely : We reserve a portion of them for our textbook adoptions… our textbook adoptions are two to $3,000,000 each.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Property tax revenue growth of 6.82% drives the FY2022–23 payment schedule, resulting in about $5.3 million for Mountain View Whisman and $3.4 million for MVLA, totaling roughly $8.7 million. He cautioned that recent slowdowns and layoffs could affect future assessed values via appeals over several years.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : For the current year… that increased by 6.82%… MVWSD receiving about $5.3 million and the high school district receiving about $3.4 million… there has been a slowdown… layoffs that could impact future assessed values… through the appeals process.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Cumulatively, the Shoreline community has paid $76.7 million to the districts since 2011 under current arrangements. Including payments going back to fiscal 2005–2006 before the current agreements, the total paid out is nearly $82 million.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : Since we’ve been doing this back in 2011 have been $76.7 million… including payments going back to fiscal 05 and 06… total of almost $82 million have been paid out.

topics.topic: Successor agreement negotiations and extension plan

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: The current Education Enhancement Reserve JPA expires June 30, 2023. City and district staff have been negotiating a successor agreement. City staff proposed a 12–18 month extension with payments at least equal to current levels (about $5.3M MVWSD and $3.4M MVLA) while completing legal and fiscal analyses.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : The… agreement… is set to expire on June 30th… I proposed the extension for 12 to 18 months… commitment during the extension would be to provide a payment at least equal to the current payments… around $5.3 million… and about $3.4 million.

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: City staff propose updating the Shoreline Area Plan to explicitly enable expanded use of tax increment for school capacity. The approach envisions a roughly 20‑year development phase (aligned with the North Bayshore Precise Plan/Google Master Plan) with growing payments, followed by long‑term flow‑through of most increment to districts and other taxing entities.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : Our legal analysis has determined… to enable expanded payments… require that the shoreline area plan be updated… over a roughly 20 year time frame… the remainder and vast majority of the… tax increment would be paid to the districts and the other taxing entities.

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: City outlined Shoreline obligations informing payment capacity: $122 million in capital improvements for sea level rise; $19 million for closed regional landfill mitigation; and $487 million in transportation projects, with about $267 million net to be funded by the Shoreline community after development impact fees and community benefits.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : $122 million in capital improvements for sea level rise… 19,000,000 in improvements for the closed regional landfill… 487,000,000 in transportation improvements… an estimated 267,000,000 are net… to be funded by the shoreline community.

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Rudolph warned that a 635‑unit project could yield about 120 students, creating roughly $2.8 million in annual operating costs at $23,000 per pupil. Absent a successor agreement, payments could stop, forcing $2.8 million in cuts as the district’s budget cycle begins in February.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : The PAIR project… 635 units… will produce 120 students… at our current expenditure of $23,000… equates to $2.8 million… if it agreement isn’t reached then… all payments will stop… we need to… start presenting a $2.8 million budget cut.

points.speaker: Audrey Ramberg
points.text: To mitigate near‑term impact, staff noted the Sobrato residential development would be available for occupancy around mid‑2024, suggesting limited immediate student enrollment effects and allowing time to reach a successor agreement providing increased support for school capacity.
points.source: Audrey Ramberg : The Sobrato development… would be available for occupation in 2024, in mid 2024… before we can get to a new successor agreement that would be substantially better equipped to meet the interests of the district.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: A public claim that the county receives its full 20% share was disputed; Takahashi clarified the county’s payment is closer to about 40% of what a full allocation would be and agreed to provide supporting documentation for review.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : They are not receiving… full… It is really closer to about 40% of what they would otherwise be getting… We can provide sort of the backup for where we’re getting our information.

topics.topic: Future meeting plan and next steps

points.speaker: Kimba McCarthy
points.text: Board members aligned on drafting a short‑term agreement guaranteeing at least current payment levels while negotiations continue, and to meet in Q1 2023 (February or early March) to review and approve it. Staff will map the process for a longer‑term successor agreement, aiming for clarity by summer 2023.
points.source: Kimba McCarthy : The Board has agreed to set a date for quarter one 2023… to discuss the short term agreement… we do intend to put forth the full amount that you all got last year… give us a little bit more time to finish the legal and the fiscal analysis.

points.speaker: Laura Blakely
points.text: A public commenter urged negotiating the successor JPA before concluding development agreements, advocating immediate allocation of districts’ full share of Shoreline tax revenues to meet a projected 50% student population increase and to build reserves, and encouraged developer contributions to infrastructure given strong corporate profits.
points.source: Laura Blakely : I urge you to… negotiate with the JPA parties first… The… student population… would increase by 50%… districts desperately need all of the tax funds… Google reported… $17.35 billion… let the schools have the money they deserve.

points.speaker: Pat Showalter
points.text: A council member stated the city’s commitment to good‑faith negotiations and emphasized sea level rise as a local responsibility, noting state and federal funds are competitive. She supported a reasonable extension to address infrastructure needs while meeting school districts’ needs through a balanced solution.
points.source: Pat Showalter : The city is committed to negotiate in good faith… We can’t do that without the sea level rise protections… it’s naive to assume that state and federal funding will be forthcoming… I urge you to consider reasonable negotiations that… involve an extension.

points.speaker: Ayinde Rudolph
points.text: Rudolph thanked city and district partners for collaborative efforts on community needs and noted ongoing joint initiatives (teacher housing, joint use). He and Chair McCarthy exchanged appreciation for cooperation and extended holiday wishes before adjournment.
points.source: Ayinde Rudolph : I just want to thank you all for your willingness… I do appreciate our continued partnership… And happy holidays by the way.

decisions.text: Draft a short‑term agreement guaranteeing at least current payment levels and outline the process for a longer‑term successor agreement, targeting presentation to the Board in Q1 2023 and process clarity by summer 2023.
decisions.source: Audrey Ramberg : That would be a pretty easy… thing to execute… we could map that… provide more clarity about the process and the timing for that.

decisions.text: Hold a Board meeting in Q1 2023 (February or early March) to review and approve the short‑term payment guarantee agreement.
decisions.source: Kimba McCarthy : The Board has agreed to set a date for quarter one 2023 for our next meeting to discuss the short term agreement.

decisions.text: Provide documentation supporting the analysis of the county’s Shoreline tax increment share for Board and district review.
decisions.source: Jesse Takahashi : We can provide sort of the backup for where we’re getting our information.
